stages.name: Discovery
stages.description: Uncover the customer's current browser environment, legacy app dependencies, security posture, and device landscape before pitching.

questions.question: How are your employees' browsers currently managed — does IT push policies centrally, or are users running whatever they like?
questions.purpose: Determine whether there's any existing browser governance — the starting point for the CEP conversation. Most orgs have zero visibility here.
followUps: Who in IT owns browser policy today?
Are browser updates handled centrally or left to individual users?
Do you have visibility into which extensions employees have installed?

industryTips.financial-services: Unmanaged browsers are a frequent finding in FCA and PCI-DSS audits — frame browser governance as a compliance gap, not just an IT preference.
industryTips.technology: Even tech-savvy teams often have blind spots here — browser-based credential harvesting and OAuth token theft target developers specifically.

questions.question: Do any of your teams rely on Windows-based or legacy applications that need a specific environment to run?
questions.purpose: Surface the Cameyo opportunity. Any Windows or legacy app dependency — Citrix, RDS, local installs — is a direct use case for app delivery in the browser.
followUps: How are those apps delivered today — Citrix, RDS, local install?
How many users need access, and on what types of devices?
What's the current cost per seat for that infrastructure — licenses, servers, IT support?

industryTips.manufacturing: Manufacturing teams almost always have legacy ERP, MES, or SCADA systems that need Windows. Ask specifically about shop floor access.
industryTips.retail: Back-office and POS management tools are often Windows-only — Cameyo delivers these to any device, including Chromebooks in stores.
industryTips.professional-services: Ask about legacy time-billing, case management, or document management systems — these are often the last reason a firm maintains Windows infrastructure.

questions.question: What's the mix of company-managed devices versus personal, unmanaged, or contractor devices in your organisation?
questions.purpose: BYOD and unmanaged endpoints are the biggest gap CEP fills. Users on personal devices are completely outside IT's control today — and most customers haven't fully reckoned with this.
followUps: How do contractors or temps access your systems?
Can IT push security controls to a personal device?
When a contractor finishes, can you revoke access to data they've already downloaded?

industryTips.retail: High contractor and seasonal staff turnover means unmanaged device access and offboarding are real risks — and often completely unaddressed.
industryTips.professional-services: Contractors accessing client data from personal devices is a liability many firms haven't fully addressed — it's a DLP conversation waiting to happen.

questions.question: Has your organisation experienced any security incidents, phishing attempts, or malware in the past 12 months — even near-misses?
questions.purpose: Establish security as a current, lived business problem rather than a theoretical risk. This is where urgency lives.
followUps: What was the impact — downtime, data loss, regulatory exposure?
Was the browser involved in any of those incidents?
Did you have visibility into what the user was doing in the browser at the time?

industryTips.financial-services: Financial services is the #1 target for phishing globally. Even a near-miss carries significant regulatory and reputational cost.
industryTips.technology: SaaS companies are increasingly targeted via browser-based credential harvesting and OAuth token theft — ask about those specifically.

questions.question: What controls do you have today to prevent sensitive data leaving via the browser — downloads to personal devices, uploads to personal cloud storage, copy-paste to external tools?
questions.purpose: DLP at the browser layer is a key CEP differentiator. Most organisations realise they have zero controls here once asked directly.
followUps: What happens if an employee copies a client list to their personal Google Drive or Dropbox?
Can IT audit what's been downloaded from your web applications?
How do you handle contractors who need to access sensitive data?

industryTips.financial-services: Browser-based DLP maps directly to PCI-DSS 3.x and FCA Consumer Duty expectations around data handling — it's a compliance investment, not just security.
industryTips.technology: Source code and IP exfiltration via personal cloud storage is a serious risk for SaaS companies — CEP's DLP controls can block this at the browser layer.

questions.question: Does your IT team have any visibility into what's happening inside the browser today — sites visited, extensions installed, file downloads, data transfers?
questions.purpose: Surface the visibility gap. IT can often see the network perimeter but is completely blind inside the browser — this is the insight that creates urgency.
followUps: If a user installs a malicious extension today, would IT know?
Is browser activity included in your SIEM or security monitoring?
How long would it take to detect a data exfiltration via the browser?

stages.name: Pitch
stages.description: Present Chrome Enterprise Premium and Cameyo as the browser-first platform that solves exactly what you uncovered in discovery. Lead with their pain, not product features.

talkingPoints.title: Chrome Enterprise Premium: the browser is your new security perimeter
talkingPoints.content: Your team already spends 80%+ of their working day in a browser. Chrome Enterprise Premium turns that surface into a zero-trust security perimeter — policy-controlled, threat-protected, and DLP-enforced. No VPN required. No new hardware. Deploys to existing Chrome browsers in hours.
tips: Lead with: 'The browser is now the #1 attack surface — yet most security budgets allocate less than 10% to it'
Ask: 'What does your current security stack cost per user vs. what CEP adds — and what's the cost of one incident?'
For Google Workspace customers: mention they may already have entitlements included

industryTips.financial-services: CEP's DLP and screen-capture controls map directly to PCI-DSS and FCA requirements — frame it as a compliance investment. Regulators are increasingly asking about browser controls.
industryTips.technology: For SaaS companies, CEP protects against OAuth token theft and credential harvesting — the #1 attack vector against tech-forward teams.

talkingPoints.title: Cameyo: your Windows apps in a Chrome tab — no Citrix, no VDI infrastructure
talkingPoints.content: Any application that runs on Windows can be delivered as a web app via Cameyo. Users open a Chrome tab, the app streams from the cloud. No servers to build or maintain. No client software to install. Typical deployment: days, not months. Customers replace Citrix at 40–60% lower TCO.
tips: If they have Citrix: get the per-seat cost before presenting Cameyo pricing — the TCO comparison is the close
Key message: 'Your users don't change anything — the Windows app just opens in a tab'
Speed angle: 'You could have 10 users running [specific app name] by end of this week'

industryTips.manufacturing: Position as a Citrix replacement for ERP and MES access — especially for shop floor workers who need one app, not a full Windows desktop.
industryTips.retail: Ideal for delivering back-office and POS management tools to Chromebooks or thin clients in stores — zero Windows infrastructure required.

talkingPoints.title: Corporate browser controls on personal and unmanaged devices — without touching the device
talkingPoints.content: For BYOD, contractor, and remote workers, CEP deploys a managed Chrome profile that enforces corporate policies within the browser, while leaving the personal side of the device completely untouched. Users get their personal Chrome profile; IT controls the work profile. The change is invisible to users; the control is real for IT.
tips: This is often the 'wow' moment for HR and legal — corporate DLP on personal devices without MDM is a novel concept for most customers
Ask first: 'What's your current policy for contractors accessing client data from their own laptop?' — silence is the answer you want

talkingPoints.title: Hours to deploy — not a 6-month infrastructure project
talkingPoints.content: CEP policy rollout to existing Chrome browsers takes hours. If the customer is on Google Workspace, many settings are pre-configured. Cameyo app onboarding is days. Compare that to a Citrix or traditional VDI rollout — which typically runs 3–9 months and requires dedicated infrastructure teams.
tips: Speed is a major differentiator vs. legacy security and virtualisation vendors
Good pilot close: 'What if we had 10 users running [specific use case] by end of next week?'
For sceptical IT buyers: low-risk pilot is more compelling than a long business case

talkingPoints.title: Works with what you already have — Google Workspace or Microsoft
talkingPoints.content: For Google Workspace customers, the Chrome Admin Console is included and may already have licences. For Microsoft-first environments, CEP integrates with Entra ID (Azure AD) via SAML/SCIM and works alongside Intune for device context. No new identity platform. No rip-and-replace.
tips: For Workspace customers: 'You may already be paying for this — let's check your entitlements'
For Microsoft shops: don't fight the stack — show how CEP augments Entra and Intune rather than replacing them
Cameyo similarly integrates with Azure AD and Okta for SSO

industryTips.technology: SaaS-native companies on Google Workspace likely already own the Chrome Admin Console — CEP is an uplift to infrastructure they're already paying for.

stages.name: Objections
stages.description: Address concerns about incumbent solutions, cost, change management, and IT complexity. Stay curious — every objection is information.

objections.objection: We already manage endpoints with Intune / SCCM / MDM
objections.response: Intune manages the device — what happens inside the browser is invisible to it. CEP fills exactly that gap: browser-layer DLP, threat protection, and policy enforcement that Intune simply can't see. They're additive, not competing. Intune handles device compliance; CEP handles what happens at the application layer. You keep everything you have and gain visibility where you currently have none.
tips: Ask: 'Can Intune tell you what your users are downloading from the web today?'
The 'additive, not competing' framing is essential — don't position as a replacement
Follow up with the visibility gap question: 'If a user installs a malicious extension tomorrow, how long until IT knows?'

objections.objection: We're a Microsoft shop — we use Edge / M365 / Azure
objections.response: Completely valid — and CEP is built for exactly this. It integrates natively with Entra ID, Intune, and Azure AD, so you're not abandoning Microsoft; you're adding a browser-layer security control that augments your existing stack. Managed Chrome and managed Edge can also coexist on the same machine for different use cases. A 30-day pilot on 10 users costs nothing to find out whether the added security layer is worth it.
tips: Never fight the Microsoft stack — always position as complementary
If Edge commitment is immovable, pivot to Cameyo as the entry point instead
Key question: 'Does managed Edge give you DLP controls at the browser layer today?'

objections.objection: We tried VDI / Citrix before and it was too complex or expensive
objections.response: That pain is exactly why Cameyo was built. Traditional VDI means servers, sizing, licensing, and months of project work. Cameyo is fully SaaS — no infrastructure to build or maintain, no upfront capital spend. Customers go from decision to users streaming apps in under a week. The complexity and cost you experienced with Citrix is the specific problem Cameyo eliminates.
tips: Validate the pain first: 'What made it painful — cost, performance, ongoing maintenance?'
Then: 'What if you could deliver the same apps with no servers and no infrastructure team?'
Offer a proof: 'We could have a test environment running in 24 hours — want to see it?'

objections.objection: My users won't change browsers / we can't force a tool change on the business
objections.response: Users don't change anything. CEP deploys as a managed profile alongside their existing Chrome install — they keep their bookmarks, extensions, history, and muscle memory. For Cameyo, the Windows app opens in a Chrome tab. Most users don't know they're in a virtual session. The change is entirely invisible to end users; the security control is entirely visible to IT. That's the point.
tips: Lead with: 'This is an IT control, not a user-facing product change'
Good closer: 'What if we ran a 10-user pilot and asked them for feedback after a week?'
For orgs with a strong Microsoft culture: point out that managed Chrome profile doesn't replace Edge — it coexists

objections.objection: We're not ready to budget for this / it's not a priority this year
objections.response: Understood — let's make sure you have the numbers to build the case when the window opens. What does your current Citrix or VDI cost per user per year, including support and infrastructure? And what's the cost of one security incident for your organisation — downtime, remediation, regulatory fine? For most customers, CEP pays for itself within the first avoided incident. We can build that TCO model together so it's ready when budget comes around.
tips: This is a 'not yet' not a 'no' — keep the deal warm
Offer a business case / TCO template as a leave-behind
Identify whether the blocker is budget cycle timing or internal priority — different solutions
Ask: 'When does your budget cycle open — and what would you need to have ready for it?'

objections.objection: We already have security covered — antivirus, firewall, VPN
objections.response: Those are network perimeter controls — they protect the edge. But most modern attacks live entirely inside the browser: phishing via legitimate-looking sites, drive-by downloads, malicious extensions, OAuth token theft. None of these trip a firewall. Antivirus can't see what a user does in a web application. CEP fills the security gap that sits between the network perimeter and the user's fingers — and it's the fastest-growing attack surface in enterprise today.
tips: Stat to use: 'The browser is involved in over 80% of enterprise security incidents — yet most security stacks are blind inside it'
Ask: 'Does your current security stack give you visibility into browser-level activity — downloads, extension installs, site visits?'
Don't dismiss their stack — acknowledge it, then show what it can't see

stages.name: Close
stages.description: Confirm the use case, identify the buying committee, and land on a clear pilot or commercial next step. The goal is a specific commitment, not a vague 'let's keep talking'.

questions.question: Based on what we've discussed, does [browser security / legacy app delivery / both] resonate as the right starting point for your organisation?
questions.purpose: Get verbal confirmation on the primary use case before moving to commercial discussion. This surfaces any remaining misalignment early.
followUps: Is there a specific team, office, or user group that would make the most compelling pilot?
Is the priority security, cost reduction, or both?
Does IT or the business own this decision?

questions.question: What would success look like for you in the first 90 days of a deployment?
questions.purpose: Anchor the conversation to measurable outcomes and surface any unstated success criteria or stakeholder expectations.
followUps: Who internally would define and measure success?
Is there a security review, IT audit, or compliance milestone this could feed into?
What would make this an easy internal sell to your IT leadership?

questions.question: Who else needs to be part of the technical evaluation or commercial conversation — IT, security, procurement, a CISO?
questions.purpose: Multi-thread the deal and identify the full buying committee before a proposal goes in. Single-threaded deals die at procurement.
followUps: Is there a CISO or IT director who would need to sign off?
Does procurement get involved at this stage or later?
Is there an internal security review or vendor evaluation process?

questions.question: Would a 30-day, 10-seat pilot help build internal confidence and give your IT team a chance to evaluate the platform hands-on?
questions.purpose: Low-friction next step that creates forward momentum and surfaces any remaining blockers in a safe, reversible way.
followUps: Which use case would you want to validate — browser security, app delivery, or both?
Who on the IT team would own the technical evaluation?
What does the internal sign-off process look like to get a pilot approved?

talkingPoints.title: Proposed pilot structure
talkingPoints.content: 10 users, 30 days, one focused use case (browser security or app delivery), evaluated by the IT owner. No infrastructure investment. Clear success criteria agreed upfront. At day 30, review against criteria and decide together on commercial pathway.
tips: Keep it simple — one use case, one team, clear criteria
Offer to write the internal business case for them if needed
Pilot momentum beats perfect business case every time for SMB

talkingPoints.title: Commercial pathway from pilot to deployment
talkingPoints.content: Pilot → internal review → commercial agreement → phased rollout. For sub-1000 seat customers, full deployment is typically achievable within 60–90 days of commercial sign-off. No professional services engagement required for standard deployments.
tips: Emphasise speed-to-value for smaller customers — this isn't an 18-month enterprise project
If procurement is involved: get them a written proposal template early
Cameyo + CEP together: position as a bundled browser platform deal, not two separate purchases
